response.justification: Bogdan Szabo is an experienced full-stack and backend engineer with a strong testing culture, but he is fundamentally misaligned with this role. The single most disqualifying factor is the 8-year C++ minimum requirement: his resume shows roughly six months of C/C++ work at National Instruments in 2014–2015 and a bachelor thesis project, with C++ absent entirely from his skills section. His primary stack is TypeScript, Node.js, and DLang. While he has touched distributed systems at scale (Dunnhumby, millions of HTTP req/s) and has some cloud exposure (AWS CDK, Kubernetes), his career arc is squarely in web/product engineering rather than systems, infrastructure, or cloud compute platforms. There is no evidence of technical leadership scoped to cloud infrastructure, and no advanced degree.
strengths: Genuine distributed systems exposure at Dunnhumby with high-throughput, low-latency micro-services handling millions of requests per second
Strong testing and engineering-practice advocacy demonstrated through community workshops, TDD methodology, and open-source testing tooling (Fluent-Asserts, Trial)
Broad cross-domain experience (web, mobile, embedded, GIS, DevOps tooling) showing adaptability and system-level thinking
concerns: C++ experience is negligible (~6 months in 2014–2015 at National Instruments), failing the 8-year minimum by a wide margin; C++ is not even listed in the skills section
Career trajectory is web and product engineering (TypeScript, Node.js, DLang), not systems programming, cloud compute platforms, or low-level infrastructure

No formal technical leadership role in a cloud or infrastructure context; leadership evidence is limited to community workshops and open-source projects rather than engineering team or platform ownership

key_factors.factor: Web/product stack focus vs. systems engineering
High-throughput distributed systems exposure at Dunnhumby
